subject: UX & Research
title: Correlation vs. Causation Traps
description: A quick, practical guide to Correlation vs. Causation Traps.

hook.text: Users who use feature X retain 40% better. Did the feature cause retention, or do loyal users use more features?

buildup.text: Correlation measures co-occurrence. Causation proves one thing caused another. Analytics shows correlation only.

discovery.text: Ice cream sales and drowning deaths correlate. Both rise in summer. Ice cream doesn't cause drowning.

twist.text: Only controlled experiments prove causation. Randomize users into groups and measure the isolated effect.

climax.text: When you can't experiment, use caution. Say 'associated with' not 'caused by' when reporting metrics.

punchline.text: Correlation isn't causation. Only experiments prove cause.

quiz.question: Why can't observational metrics prove that a feature causes retention?
options: Correlation doesn't prove causation — loyal users may just use more features naturally
Retention can only be measured with surveys
